candidates.stmt:
Natalie Kane is a trade unionist, access specialist and heritage worker.

“Brixton has a unique history, and deserves an equitable, fair and progressive future.For the past few years, I’ve worked on disability justice and design inequalities.

I’ve lived in Brixton for almost a decade, and I’ve seen the impact that the removal of funding for public services has had on people’s lives. Failures to build affordable, accessible housing have devastated Lambeth. I believe strongly in community and collective power, and so I look forward to working with residents to hear what work we can do together.”

David Bridson lives in the ward and has called Brixton home for over 10
years. He proudly serves as a councillor for Brixton Acre Lane. David is
Lambeth’s Cabinet Member for Healthier Communities, leading work to tackle
health inequalities, strengthen adult social care and support sanctuary
seekers rebuilding their lives in our borough. He also leads the council’s work
tackling rough sleeping, addiction and mental health. Outside his councillor
role, David works for a national youth charity campaigning to build more
genuinely affordable homes for young people and expand youth services.
He also serves as a governor at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital. David is
standing in order to keep delivering every day for Brixton Acre Lane ward.

Sarbaz came to Lambeth as a teenager, and this became his home ever since. He is passionate about making a difference in the environment, homelessness, social care, and youth services. During the pandemic, Sarbaz helped provide hot meals at local hospitals to NHS staff and took the charitable initiative to provide accommodation and food to refugees.

Dario Goodwin is an inequality and climate campaigner.

“Communities are being excluded. We should be working together.

I’m a union activist, climate campaigner, and local renter. I’ve seen how much our communities have been put through and now billionaires are thriving while foodbank queues break records every year. Meanwhile, Labour just wait to lose to Reform. But I’ve also seen the strength in Brixton’s communities coming together. I know we can build a better future.”

I am standing as an independent candidate because I believe Brixton deserves representatives who act, not just promise.

Over the past few weeks, I have worked alongside local traders, residents and organisers to support the campaign at Brixton Plaza, where 27 small businesses were given just one month’s notice to leave. By listening, building trust and working quickly with legal experts, we helped secure a High Court injunction protecting those traders from immediate eviction.

For me, this is what local politics should look like: being present, understanding people’s lived realities, and turning that into action that delivers real outcomes.

Brixton is not short of community, creativity or resilience — but too often decisions are made without people, rather than with them. I want to change that by bringing decision-making closer to residents, supporting local businesses, and ensuring people are treated fairly and with respect.

I am not part of a political party. I am accountable to the people I represent, and I will always prioritise the needs of the community over party lines.

If elected, I will continue to show up, listen, and work relentlessly to get things done for Brixton.

Lee has lived in Clapham Town for 27 years. His career spans live entertainment and broadcast media including launching The O2 as Commercial Director. He now runs an environmental and marketing agency. Lee is Chair of Governors for a secondary academy, Board Director of the Royal Edinburgh Military Tattoo, a Committee Member of the Clapham Society, and is particularly active in local transport campaigns.

Nanda is a life-long Lambeth resident, who works in the NHS and is a school governor. As a councillor, she has fought to help reduce poverty, inequality, and pollution, by improving services and creating new opportunities, for all residents, especially our children and young people, and those from protected characteristics.

I have been a nurse for 30 years, and a Homeless Health Nurse for 22 of those. I have expertise in housing, immigration and health rights advocacy through my job and believe that I can use this effectively to support the residents of Brixton North.
I am passionate about the need for decent social housing, which is truly accessible for people with all types of diabilities, and sustainable from an energy perspective. I really understand the need to get the Council and landlords to respond quickly to housing issues like black mould and repairs. Special Educational Needs support in Lambeth needs to improve, and I would fight for this. I want to see street litter elimated, and local environments improved for everyone - there are some great green spaces within Brixton North, but these can always be improved. I think GP health access needs to be addressed urgently, and improvements in mental health support should be a priority. I am passionate about reducing stigma and discrimmination in all it's forms. I have lived on 2 council estates in Lambeth over the last 18 years, with my partner, two boys (aged 15 and 16), a ginger cat and 3 goldfish. I love my (small!) garden, and watching a bit of Sci Fi with my boys. I've previously been involved in community gardening projects and I do community cooking at Oasis on a Saturday.My best quality is that I always do I what I say I am going to do...!

John-Paul is a community organiser who has lived in the ward his whole life. He’s worked to bring genuinely affordable housing to Lambeth in the form of Community Land Trusts, and worked on youth safety and air pollution as part of Lambeth Citizens.John-Paul will aim to engage and bring residents together to tackle issues.

**A message to the electors of Brixton North**

I'm **Eduardo Salgado, your Councillor for Brixton North.**

I'm not a career politician. I'm a public transport worker, a coordinator for SolidarioGB, and a community activist. I'm a socialist and an anti-imperialist — not because of ideology alone, but because I've lived and worked alongside people who are too often forgotten by those in power.

My commitment to you is simple: I will always give voice to those the system ignores. That means standing up for migrants, for the marginalised, for low-income families, for renters, for workers — for anyone in Brixton North who feels invisible.

In the council chamber and on our streets, I will fight for:
- Decent, affordable housing for all
- Better public transport, not privatisation
- Real support for migrant communities, not hostile policies
- Stronger community action, not empty promises

Politics has turned its back on working-class communities for too long. Brixton North deserves better.

I don't make promises I can't keep. But I promise this: I will listen, I will act, and I will never stop fighting for you.

Yours in solidarity,

*_Eduardo Salgado_*
Councillor for Brixton North

Resident in Lambeth for 24 years, Marcia’s career covers social care, safeguarding children and young adults. She volunteers as a mentor, supporting young people affected by crime. Marcia is a former chaplain and board member of the West Indian Association of Service Personnel. A public governor of Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, Marcia has extensive charitable experience and for 15 years ran a not-for-profit enterprise for Lambeth’s homeless.

Clapham Town Conservatives' key policies are: - Lower Council Tax - Protect the Common - Re-open Larkhall Bridge - Fight for Lambeth leaseholders and tenants - Cleaner streets

Lambeth Conservatives' full manifesto is available at https://www.lambethconservatives.org.uk/news/lambeth-conservatives-2022-manifesto

candidates.p: The Socialist Party of Great Britain
candidates.stmt: Anya Krycek is an Irish-born radical feminist and socialist theoretician who has lived in South London for 21 years, bringing together deep expertise in feminist studies with committed revolutionary politics as a member of the Socialist Party of Great Britain. A regular contributor to The Socialist Standard  she synthesizes radical feminist analysis with impossiblism and orthodox Marxist theory to examine the interconnected systems of patriarchy and capitalism, rejecting reformist approaches in favour of fundamental social transformation. Her political work is grounded in practical community engagement, including hands-on animal rescue volunteering that aligns with her ethical framework a life long strict vegetarian, and she is currently putting these principles into electoral practice as the SPGB candidate for Brixton North in the May 2026 local elections, where her two decades of embedded local knowledge inform her advocacy for the ward's residents

Zvikomborero Chihoro is standing to represent Rush Common ward because he believes local people should have a real voice in the decisions that shape their community. He has lived in Lambeth for three years and calls it home. He moved here from Birmingham with his best friend of 18 years, and though he lost him last year, the community around him only made him feel even more rooted here.

He loves Brixton’s diversity and the everyday connections that make it special, from local businesses to walking his two dogs in Brockwell Park. He wants to make sure everyone can enjoy the park, as well as the events and festivals held there, without being priced out.

Zvikomborero has spent his career in social value and social impact, helping businesses create opportunities for young people and those facing barriers to work. He wants to bring that experience to support residents, protect green spaces, and make Brixton a place where everyone belongs.

Sacha Takyi-Berko is a local activist for better housing and more support for children, especially children with special educational needs. She has worked with local special educational charities and support providers like Berridge Road Resource Centre and other tutoring programs to reach our most vulnerable and disenfranchised children.
 
As a parent of a disabled and special needs young person, Sacha is well aware of the pressures faced by parents and families – and of the failures of our services. She is passionate about holding local authorities to account on these, as well as changing the wider system that is failing us.

She is a long standing activist for social justice and intends to champion safe homes, adaptions and housing security. All residents deserve to be treated with dignity – not as case numbers.

Viktor Westerdahl has lived in London for 20 years, mostly in rented poor quality accommodation, experiencing mould, vermin and no-fault eviction first hand. He is now a qualified architect and small business owner who lives in the Rush Common ward. Viktor cares deeply about housing and its potential to transform lives, as well as the harm a substandard and insecure home can cause.

Viktor has worked as an architect on housing-led schemes for councils across London which has provided him with hands on experience in the planning and delivery of mixed use housing schemes. As an elected representative, he will bring this expertise in-house to some of Lambeth’s central challenges; where and how to build new homes, how to refurbish existing estates and critically how to do so together with, and for the benefit of local residents.

Viktor also has experience in community-based work. As part of an artist collective, he helped establish a social enterprise that supports young people from underrepresented backgrounds to enter creative courses at university. He opposes neocolonial wars and the ongoing genocide in Gaza and will push for the council to disinvest accordingly.

Marcia grew up and went to school in Brixton Rush Common Ward and has been proud to represent the area since 2006. As Lambeth's cabinet Member for Economic Inclusion, she has helped lead Labour's work to spread opportunity Locally - supporting over 300 businesses to become Living Wage accredited and expanding apprenticeships so more residents can access fair pay and good jobs. Locally, Marcia is known for persistent casework and community campaigning - from improving bus services and creating new play areas to supporting local greening projects and neighbourhood groups. She works closely with residents, through regular surgeries and community meetings, to push for practical improvements across the ward.

Ben was born in Streatham and now lives in Brixton Rush Common ward with his family. As Lambeth's cabinet Member for Children, Young People and Families, he leads Labour's work to make Lambeth the best place for young people to grow up-expanding breakfast clubs, strengthening children's services and increasing investment in SEND places in Lambeth schools by £10 million. Locally, Ben campaigns with residents on issues such as road safety, housing and enhancing community spaces like Elm Park and Tulse Hill Adventure Playground. Residents often meet him at regular police and neighbourhood meetings where he works with them to get
problems fixed and improvements delivered.

Adrian has represented the Brixton area for 16 years and currently serves as Mayor of Lambeth. Known for his thorough casework and strong community presence, Adrian regularly attends residents' meetings, estate forums and neighbourhood groups to ensure local voices shape council decisions. As Mayor he has championed Local charities including Age uK Lambeth and Lambeth & Croydon Foodbank, while supporting community events across the borough. Adrian has also worked closely with residents on issues such as Rush common improvements, Brockwell Park matters and Local service delivery.

I have lived in Rush Common ward since 2019 and I know the community and its concerns very well.

LTNs need to be reviewed on a case by case basis. They were forced on everyone without taking into consideration the community’s needs or opinion. This is unfair.

We need to restore democracy to Lambeth Council which has been ruled by Labour for years without accountability or oversight.

1) I will work on improving cycling infrastructure by adding a speed camera on Effra road (so that the 20 mph speed limit means 20 mph).

2) I will work for a new cycle lane without affecting traffic to help cyclists avoid being in the middle of a three-lane road when they join the A23 from Effra road.

3) St Matthew’s Community Hall has been dead for years. I will seek support from the government and businesses to create regular activities for our young to provide them with new skills in IT and coding as well as art and culture.

Carlotta Allum is the founder of STRETCH, a local criminal justice charity that helps people who’ve committed offences or are at risk reintegrate into society through creative arts and culture. She works with marginalised and excluded communities, who are most harmed by our failing social safety net.

She’s an artist and activist, as well as a mother of three. Carlotta is hugely passionate about activities for young people to keep them away from crime.

Lambeth Council and Labour have failed the people of Brixton – Carlotta strongly believes in a people-led borough where local residents make can make a real a difference.

Serafina Spicer has lived in Lambeth for the past five years and is standing in Brixton Windrush because she believes local people should have a real say over the decisions that shape their homes and neighbourhoods.

After facing steep and confusing charges in her own estate, Serafina founded and now leads a residents’ group bringing together three buildings to demand transparency and accountability from their housing provider. What began as conversations between neighbours has grown into organised meetings, detailed evidence-gathering and collective action. She has seen how quickly people can be dismissed when they act alone, and how powerful they are when they act together.

Serafina wants a council that backs residents, protects genuinely affordable housing and treats community groups as partners rather than obstacles. For her, Brixton’s strength lies in its people and in making sure they are heard.

I moved to Brixton in 1991, learned to swim at the Rec, studied at Brixton College, danced at the Lambeth Country Show and ran 5K round Brockwell Park.

Over the last four years as a Labour councillor, I have got to know the wealth of organisations in Brixton and actively supported the work that they do, from Southwyck Pantry to Brixton Library and the Street Gym to the Lambeth Schools Patrol. I regularly join the litter pick atLoughborough Park and throughout the pandemic I packed food parcels at the Food Hub in Brixton Rec.

I have been a councillor for Coldharbour, and previously Angell Ward, since 1998. It was a great honour to become Mayor of Lambeth in 2015, after being Deputy Mayor in 2014. I have held several Cabinet roles, including Housing, Adult Social Care and Health, Community Safety and Partnerships. I currently lead on the Voluntary and Community Sector, Leisure and Sports. As well as being a councillor, I have been a senior manager of several third sector organisations, including a national HIV charity.

Hello, 
I’m Joanne. I’m running because I want to help strengthen our community and make sure residents feel heard and supported. I’ve lived in Windrush Ward for 22 years, raised my two children here, and this community is home. Everything I do is rooted in care, compassion and the belief that real change starts with being heard.

For the past few years, I’ve been running a CIC called When We Talk We Heal, creating open mics and creative healing workshops across Lambeth to give people space to express, connect and grow. That work has shown me the power of community, the importance of inclusion, and how much people can achieve when they’re given the chance to speak and be listened to.

I’ve spent time listening to what matters to people here, and I’m committed to working collaboratively to improve services and opportunities. I stand for transparency, community power, true democracy, and putting residents’ needs at the centre of decision‑making.

I’m committed to being a champion for Windrush Ward someone who understands the community because I’m part of it, and someone who will always stand up for the voices that deserve to be at the centre.

I came to London 21 years ago from France and had the full London experience, first as a student benefiting from its world-renowned universities, then as a mother raising three children and now as a professional in the higher education sector living in Brixton in the Windrush ward.

I began campaigning in 2016 to remain in the EU and then joined the Liberal Democrats after the referendum, as the only party which want to give the people the final say on the Brexit deal. As a pro-EU campaigner, I am particularly concerned about the implications of Brexit for local businesses and its impact on our communities. My previous job in the private sector made me well aware of the importance of promoting the local economy as it benefits services and makes people's lives better. I was proud to be the Liberal Democrat London Assembly candidate for Lambeth and Southwark in the London 2021 elections, pleased to be able to bring my expertise on education and children's services to the role.

I have lived in London for 10 years, the day I became a citizen, I knew I wanted to give something back to the community that welcomed me. I joined the Liberal Democrats because their core values—tolerance, inclusion, and LGBTQ+ rights—are my values.
I am standing for Brixton Windrush because I believe our neighbourhood deserves better. For too long, the local Council has felt distant and disconnected. After the challenges of Brexit and the ongoing cost-of-living crisis, Brixton is struggling, and the current leadership has been absent when we needed them most.
I bring with me a lifelong passion for community organising, which began in my youth in Rome. I want to bridge the gap between the people of Brixton and the Town Hall.
My priorities for Brixton Windrush are:
• A Present Council: Creating a direct line of communication between residents and decision-makers.
• Equality & Inclusion: Championing the rights of our diverse community and protecting LGBTQ+ spaces.
• Local Recovery: Supporting our high streets and community hubs that have been neglected post-Brexit.
I want to be your voice, listen to your concerns, and be on the frontline to solve them. Let’s build a fairer, more connected Brixton together.

Simon Cordon moved to Lambeth in 1998 and has lived in Clapham for the past 16 years. He has served as a councillor elsewhere including as social care lead – where he pioneered involving people in designing their own services, such as ensuring children in care were included in shaping their own care plans. Locally, he sits on a Policing Oversight Board and has chaired Lambeth’s Leaseholders’ Council.

Simon worked in manufacturing and then in the charitable sector with the Terrence Higgins Trust. Between 2012-24, as operational head of London wide NHS sexual assault services, he oversaw the creation of the Capital’s first comprehensive NHS service for sexually abused children. He provided expert evidence to the Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse regarding the shocking historic abuse in Lambeth’s children’s homes.

He advised the Scottish Government during their legislative response to child sexual assault, has sat on Met Police strategy (‘Gold’) groups, chaired a London-wide criminal justice partnership for 12 years and co-designed a statutory / third sector partnership for survivors.

Simon cares about injustice — whether international atrocities or pressures facing young people through barriers to home ownership or Brexit‑imposed limits on travel and work in the EU. Locally he believes in practical action. He contacted the police to propose a way to prioritise reviewing residents’ CCTV footage within existing resources — a change they agreed to. He is developing a plan to increase weekend litter collection on Clapham Common within current budgets.

Simon enjoys walking the Capital Ring with his partner and composes music as a hobby. “One reason I am standing is to counter Lambeth’s culture of ‘tick box consultations’ and ‘Town Hall knows best’. I want to use my experience of involving people in the decisions that affect them to champion a resident-first, resident-led Council.”

Clapham Common & Abbeville has been my home for 25 years and where I raised my family. It’s here that I walk, exercise, shop and meet my neighbours every day. I love living here and want to play my part in making the community as great as possible.

I volunteer locally at the Lambeth Foodbank Hub and have been overwhelmed by the generosity of our wonderful community. I look forward to working together with you in the ward as we move forward.

Shao-Lan has lived in Lambeth since 2012, been a local government officer since 2017 and ran as local councillor in 2022. She has had a varied career in education and public service, including in the Galapagos Islands and helping refugees and asylum seekers to find English classes. She’s vegan, a yogi and a family celebrant who loves salsa dancing.

She is appalled by the atrocities and inhumanity we’ve witnessed in Palestine. Starmer and Sunak have both failed by refusing to call out war crimes.

Shao-Lan says: “I’m a Green because of the party’s progressive policies and bold stance on social justice”

candidates.p: The Socialist Party of Great Britain
candidates.stmt: Long time member of SPGB

I'm excited to be your Labour candidate for Torbay!   My journey is shaped by diverse experiences and I'm eager to bring my unique background to serve Torbay. Labour has a positive vision to make seaside towns great again and with your support, we can help Torbay fulfil its potential.    Labour will reform business rates, restore bathing water quality in the bay and boost tourism to help Torbay thrive.    As an accountant, I have worked with businesses and seen first hand the damage the Tories have done to our economy. A Labour government would prioritise economic stability with fully costed pledges. I understand what makes businesses tick and will work closely with a Labour Government to bring much-needed investment into Torbay.    VOTE FOR CHANGE. VOTE FOR LABOUR.

Joanna has taught in London schools for 40 years working first in secondary schools and then with children with a wide range of additional needs. She now works mostly on a voluntary basis with families in need via several Lambeth-based charities.

She has lived in Clapham Park for about 30 years and is actively engaged with the Green Party and with other groups in Lambeth-wide campaigning issues such as housing and the environment.

Joanna's other campaigning work is local to Clapham Park. She has initiated and driven changes to street verge mowing practices to encourage greater biodiversity specifically in Clarence Avenue. This was a slow process and involved negotiating with Lambeth Parks and working with Plantlife.org.uk.

Her local environmental group 'Clarence Avenue Growers' has 37 members. They have also set up the Triangle Garden with book library, composting and food growing with plans for more educational activities to follow over the summer.

Joanna is the neighbourhood champion for her area and as such she is continuing to try to materially improve her area - litter picking, putting up nesting boxes, asking for street cleaning/rubbish to be removed more regularly are a small part of this.

Although little can be done since planning was given by Lambeth Council for the current phase of the Clapham Park 'regeneration', she has vigorously campaigned to save local street trees. Joanna and others met with the developers to question the strategy for tree removal which involves about 350 mature trees across the whole area of development, and to improve communication with local residents in the future. This work is ongoing.

Joanna has also been concerned about the loss of local shops in the planning for Clapham Park Estate and set up the campaign to save local shops on Poynders Corner - although the campaign was not successful, it did create awareness and built on existing community action for positive change.

Louie grew up in Brixton Hill and is proud to still call the area home. Of mixed Irish and Black Caribbean heritage, Louie values the diversity and strong community spirit that make Lambeth special. 
Louie lives in Metropolitan Thames Valley social housing and understands first-hand the issues many residents face, from housing repairs to the rising cost of living. Louie works supporting children with special educational needs, helping young people and families overcome challenges.
As a Labour and Co-operative Party candidate, Louie wants to give back to the community that raised him and work with residents to improve housing, safer streets and opportunities for local families.

Harry O’Donoghue is an Irish immigrant. Having lived and worked in Lambeth for the past few years, he has been nominated to run in the Clapham Park ward.

He is deeply involved in the Irish community and with causes such as Palestinian solidarity.

A founder of Irish in Britain against political policing, he believes the people of Lambeth deserve better than this incompetent Labour one-party state.

In Ireland, he has seen That Community assemblies have been used successfully to drive major change and are the best local tool to hold your representatives to account.

I’ve been a Clapham Town councillor for 12 years, raised my family here and been a resident since 1976. It’s been a privilege to support our community groups, including as a Trustee of Omnibus Theatre since it was saved for community use in 2013.

Supporting thousands of residents over many years, my proudest moments have been assisting the most vulnerable. This stands alongside our record to save Clapham Fire Station and Clapham Post Office, and secure the redevelopment of the Old Town and Venn Street. With this experience, I promise to continue fighting for our community.

I’m proud to be standing in Clapham Town Ward, the place I was born and still call home. I’ve been an active community campaigner, working alongside our local councillors David and Linda to deliver for residents.

As a younger resident, I understand the pressures facing our community. I’ve helped lead successful campaigns to save our Post Office, boost local police provision and strengthen our High Street. Professionally, I work in purpose-led campaigns, helping charities and environmental organisations win change. I’ll be a visible, responsive councillor fighting for a greener, safer and more equal Clapham.

Jonathan Bartley grew up in Clapham and has lived in the local area all his life. He previously served as a Green Party councillor, successfully tackling fly-tipping and anti-social behaviour while working to protect Lambeth’s green spaces.

For four years he led the Green opposition on Lambeth Council, standing up to cuts to vital local services and exposing the council's corruption and waste.

Jonathan is also a foster carer. A former co-leader of the national Green Party, he continues to campaign for fairer communities, with a particular focus on defending the rights of disabled people and refugees.

Jonathan's other passion is protecting live music. As a musician himself he was nominated blues drummer of the year, and has played festivals all around the country including Glastonbury and recently at the 02 Shepherd's Bush.

It has been a real privilege to be one your local councillors here in Clapham Town, the ward I have lived in for over two decades.

Since being elected, I have been a visible and prominent voice who has consistently stood up for residents by protecting local services such as the Post Office, securing more police officers on the streets and championing new initiatives like the Reimagined scheme on Voltaire Road.

I am determined to build on this work and continue securing wins for all our residents, to make our community the best place to live.

Clapham needs a local voice!

Mark Bittlestone is a standup comedian who has lived in and run comedy nights in Brixton and Clapham for a decade. He has seen the area struggle first hand as more and more funding has been cut by central government. A homelessness epidemic, rubbish filling the streets, ordinary people and cultures who built the area priced out, while developers are supported by Lambeth’s Labour Council in their quest to bulldoze its historic centres. Teaching History to Sixth Form students at a school in Vauxhall, Mark saw the real-life impact of rampant inequality in a borough where magnificent townhouses stand cheek-by-jowl with widespread poverty and child homelessness.

Israel’s genocide in Gaza tore a hole in his conception of the world - a world he didn’t find very funny anymore. He felt an unabashed, unrelenting fury that this could happen, 80 years on from The Holocaust, 80 years from when we collectively said Never Again. Howling about it on social media and raising funds for Medical Aid for Palestinians failed to abate his rage and, realising the interconnected nature of struggle, he decided to channel this fury into fighting local injustice. If elected, he will work tirelessly to improve ordinary people’s lives and undo the damage done by 16 years of unrelenting austerity and 40 years of a profit-first housing system.

I’m standing as a Conservative candidate in Streatham Hill East, alongside Thomas Gray.

My key priorities are to remove the unfair LTN which pushes traffic and pollution onto boundary roads, stand up for Leigham Court Estate residents to get housing repairs done in a timely fashion, and lower council taxes through staff sharing arrangements with other local boroughs.

Our full manifesto can be read at: https://www.lambethconservatives.org.uk/sites/www.lambethconservatives.org.uk/files/2022-04/Lambeth%20Manifesto.pdf

Or you can reach out to me via twitter

Pete is an active local campaigner on issues facing Gipsy Hill. He has championed residents of Central Hill Estate where he lived for 7 years. As a Lambeth councillor from 2018 - 2022 he helped get London’s first Climate Emergency motion passed, campaigned to ban glyphosate weedkiller, pressed for divestment from fossil fuels and for better air pollution monitoring and action in the borough.

Pete is an engineer and project manager who spent 22 years in the military and a further 10 years working for Central Government. Throughout this time he has seen how money is wasted by the Tory and Labour governments. He’s appalled at the savagery and inhumanity of the Palestine conflict. He supports the Green Party’s call for fair treatment of migrants and refugees and will drive for fairness and equality in all decisions.

Pete says “Labour have effectively bankrupted Lambeth Council. Much needed services are being forced to shut and residents who depend on these services the most, are effectively being forced away from their homes and communities. Schools are closing and the gap between the 'haves' and 'have nots' is widening. We need to open Lambeth's books and ensure that decisions are made transparently and that lessons are learned from past mistakes"

"Elections over the last 10 years have shown us that Gipsy Hill will be a straight fight between Labour and The Green Party. We need more opposition councillors to hold Labour to account. Vote Green to press for real meaningful change for all of us and our future generations.”

Chloe Hawryluk is a former parish councillor and long-time activist dedicated to making Lambeth a fairer, greener borough. She’s an elected representative on the National Executive of the Young Greens, and works for a pro-European organisation, campaigning to repair Britain’s relationship with Europe.

As a young working class woman, Chloe understands first-hand the pressures many residents face. From rising rents and insecure housing, to the lasting impact of austerity and the cost of living crisis, her experiences reflect those of many people across Gipsy Hill. As a renter herself, she knows how difficult it is for young people and families to settle down in their own community.

Chloe is committed to standing up for the diverse communities of Gipsy Hill, fighting for affordable housing, accessible public services, and a greener, healthier neighbourhood. She believes local politics should work for everyone, and is determined to ensure that the voices of ordinary residents are heard loud and clear.

I’m Robert Shorrock and I'm proud to be standing as your Gipsy Hill Labour candidate.

Originally from Wigan, I have worked as a lawyer in London for many years. I have also worked as a caseworker for a Labour MP where I supported people with immigration claims, housing issues, and concerns about crime. As a renter in London for almost nine years, I know the unfairness of the system, and the need for renters' rights and new sustainable homes. I am passionate about fighting to make streets safer for everyone and have previously volunteered for a charity supporting those experiencing loneliness and vulnerability.

If elected I will build on the excellent work of Rebecca Spencer, and work closely with Christine Banton to be a visible, accessible councillor who listens and champions Gipsy Hill every step of the way.

Paul Valentine has lived in Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction for 10 years. He is an actor, theatre worker and tour guide in and around London.

Paul has a long history of involvement with Trade Unions, having been on the Executive Council of Equity as well as founding Equity for a Green New Deal – a UK Wide Union network formed to greenify the creative industries. He is also very active in the local branch structure and is on the Equity London South branch committee. Paul was previously Chair of PCS at Southbank Centre and lead the Union Branch throughout tumultuous times during the pandemic.

As only the second Green to ever be elected to Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction he is looking forward to further immersing himself in our wonderful community.

Deepak was born and brought up in Herne Hill, is a Trustee of the Friends of Ruskin Park, active in Herne Hill Welcomes Refugees and works in international development for the UN. If elected Deepak will work for new affordable homes for residents and a cleaner greener Herne Hill and Loughborough Junction for all.

Marianna Masters has been selected to stand as Labour's Parliamentary candidate for St Neots and Mid Cambridgeshire.

Marianna is a councillor and has plenty of experience at a national and local level of leadership. She is the Chair of the Fabian Society's Fabian Women's Network.



Currently a hospital governor, Marianna has campaigned locally and nationally on mental health. She has worked to raise awareness of how health inequalities, social and racial injustice affect too many people in our communities.



Speaking after their selection, Marianna Masters said:



"I'm delighted to be chosen to stand for Labour in St Neots and Mid Cambridgeshire.



"Whether living in the most rural parts of the constituency, in our market town of St Neots or on the outskirts of Cambridge - people of all ages across St Neots and mid Cambridgeshire have witnessed a decline in local services. On the doorsteps I hear about difficulties accessing GP appointments, reductions in local bus services and a lack of properly affordable housing in both towns and villages.



"Only a Labour government can deliver the change that St Neots and Mid Cambridgeshire needs. I will give voice to the people of this constituency, and I am delighted to campaign for a Labour government to restore hope and opportunity in this community."

My priorities for Finchley and Golders Green: •	Safety on our streets: tackling antisocial behaviour and antisemitism.  •	Building family homes: considering their impact on infrastructure and community services. •	Supporting local businesses: fostering an environment for them to flourish.  •	Standing up for families: and against the proposed imposition of VAT on independent schools.  •	Putting you at the heart of our NHS: ensuring residents shape local services.

David has lived in Kennington for twenty years and has been the local councillor for Kennington and Vauxhall (Prince’s ward) since 2014. David has spent most of his career working in the NHS. David works hard for local residents, providing representation and taking up a wide range of issues and concerns. These have included reducing fly-tipping, getting graffiti removed and sorting out housing matters.

Michael was born in Waterloo and has lived the majority of his life in North Lambeth. As a council tenant he was chair of the Vauxhall Neighbourhood Housing Forum and of Lambeth Tenants’ Council. As a parent he was the Chair of Governors at Pimlico School, and is currently a governor of Hitherfield School.

He has led many campaigns, from stopping two Council proposals to develop Lambeth Walk Green in 2000, to getting a new Jubilee Gardens on the South Bank managed by a community trust, opened by the Queen in 2012. Michael led the campaign to stop the Garden Bridge, lodging a Judicial Review of the Boris Johnson folly - Boris promised it would be paid from private donations, but ended up costing the public purse £47m with nothing to do show for the money!

For the past 20 years he has run Waterloo Community Development Group, and most recently represented Lambeth Village at the Albert Embankment public inquiry, where they successfully persuaded the Secretary of State to overturn Lambeth Council’s support for four tower blocks.

Since becoming effectively a one-party state in 2014, Lambeth has stopped listening to communities, rational argument or due process. The result is principles upended, policy ignored, meagre funds wasted and social injustice multiplied. No party has the monopoly on wisdom. Michael is standing to help retrieve pluralism for Lambeth.

Lambeth is at the epi-centre of London's development industry, the biggest industry in London. But much development is unnecessary, delivers no public benefit, and merely creates a haven for off-shore investment and vacant buildings, which generate millions of tonnes of CO2 for no discernible purpose.

Vote for Michael Ball. Vote for a kinder Kennington, a fairer Lambeth, and a sustainable planet.

KJ Eastwood is trying to serve the cause of the every-person and the most disenfranchised. Standing up to bullies as a child has translated into standing up to global injustice in protests and volunteering with Black Lives Matter, Palestine Solidarity Campaign and Stand Up To Racism. KJ believes that now it’s time to bring that spirit into Lambeth Council. He has worked to coordinate efforts to pick up litter where the local Labour council has failed to employ adequate public health precautions particularly on densely populated estates. He has also co-run a monthly meet up for all Trans and Gender Non-Conforming folks to have time to wind down and enjoy some aspect of life. Like many of those reading this, KJ is fed up with the needs of the many being swept aside for the desires of the few.

With £33m being wasted on keeping council homes empty, there is no reasonable justification not to demand these homes be filled with those in the need, and the money being wasted be poured into reopening closed down GP surgeries and provided community spaces where people can connect and foster support and hope rather than fear and division.  A better world is possible, as is a better London, but within our control so is a better Kennington, for all.

This isn’t done overnight, but so much can be changed in such a short period of time, looking across the pond at the results of the new Mayor of New York City proves just this. People don’t want to be promised a distant future of change, but a brighter today and tomorrow. We will do all we can to supply that.

James O’Nions has spent his adult life campaigning for a world where people come before profit.

From privatisation to climate change, the power of big business and billionaires is a disaster for ordinary people. James works for an organisation that challenges that power, often in solidarity with people in Africa, Asia and Latin America. But as a resident of Lambeth for 12 years, he wants to make a difference closer to home too. That means opposing the way massive developers are reshaping the borough in the interests of the rich, and making sure everyone in Lambeth is treated with dignity and respect.

He’s passionate about making Lambeth a wildlife-friendly borough, with public space that improves the quality of our lives. But he’s also angry that local government funding has been decimated by successive governments, including Labour, hitting the most vulnerable hardest while making life worse for everyone. If elected, he would work with Green councillors around the country to push for  spending cuts to be reversed.

My name is Stevan Cirkovic and I’m running for the council because I believe Kennington deserves serious, hands-on representation - working with the whole community, not taking it for granted. If elected as your councillor, I will work hard to:

- Tackle the housing crisis, rogue landlords, empty flats and demand reliable and timely repair in council homes
- Protect green spaces, clean air and water across the ward and borough
- Give residents and communities greater democratic control over local decisions and budgets
- Stand up for openness and diversity in our borough, its many communities, and against all forms of racism, sexism, and discrimination
- Protect and grow community spaces and access to vital public services, including post offices, GP surgeries, and an NHS embedded in our neighbourhoods.

I know how to turn public funding into real results. In my day job, I lead large-scale investment programmes in health infrastructure - taking big funding commitments and making sure they deliver in practice. That’s the approach I’ll bring to the council. Too often, we see headline announcements without delivery. I focus on turning plans into outcomes - with clear standards, value for money, and genuine co-design with communities. In the middle of a housing crisis, Kennington needs more than promises. We need credible plans to build more social housing, enforce proper standards for repairs and safety, and ensure public money creates real assets for our community, not just developers. I will also push for stronger protections for renters and take action against rogue landlords who flout the rules.

Labour’s culture of ‘town hall knows best’ and tick-box consultations have failed to deliver for residents across the Borough. Instead, it has landed us with hefty bills for empty spaces and lawsuits. My Liberal Democrat colleagues and I want to reset how the Borough works with residents and as a community organiser I know participation must be meaningful. In 2022, I organi

Elizabeth Fraser has lived on Cressingham Gardens Estate since 2003. She graduated with a Bachelors degree in animation and illustration in 2025 and works as a creative arts technician at a college.

Lambeth Council has been planning to demolish the 306 homes on her estate for nearly 15 years, which has caused immense grief and uncertainty. Elizabeth’s introduction to activism was at 13-years-old when she first spoke at Lambeth Town Hall against the demolition proposal. Since then, she has seen and experienced the instability caused by Lambeth Council’s attempts at social cleansing in her community and other estates in Lambeth, and has been spreading awareness of this issue using her social media platform.

Lisa Schulkind moved to Brixton in 1990, fell in love with it, and stayed for 40 years, working and bringing up three children in Brixton and nearby.

She worked for many years as a caseworker, (at Centre 70 in West Norwood and South London Refugee Association in Streatham) doing advice and casework, supporting Lambeth residents with housing, benefit debt and other issues. Currently, she teaches English to migrants in a community setting in Tulse Hill.

Watching the impact on ordinary people of lack of regulation in housing, the erosion of the welfare state, the hostile environment, as well as incompetence and corruption in local government is what made her furious enough to want to try to change the system so that we look after the needs of people and families rather than private capital.

Matt Wilcock‘s journey into local action began during the 2020 lockdown, eventually leading him to the Bzz Community Garden by West Norwood Bus Garage in 2021. Since then, Matt has founded “Leaves Breathe,” a social enterprise that supports residents across 24 community gardens in London –  such as council estates, playgrounds, and homes for the elderly. His work has seen these grassroots groups evolve far beyond planting; these groups also launch independent allotments, organised vital social events for isolated individuals, spearheaded food bank drives, and built a powerful network of peer-to-peer mentorship.

Matt is running for Knight’s Hill because he believes that when a community finds its voice – in a garden or beyond –  it gains the momentum to tackle the ward’s biggest challenges. Matt wants a council that acts as an enabler, providing direct representation and increased funding for Tenants and Residents Associations (TRAs) and local groups. For Matt, these organisations are the backbone of a resilient neighbourhood. By building “social muscle” at the street level, he aims to ensure that Knight’s Hill isn’t just managed from Brixton, but is empowered to lead its own future with the resources it deserves.

Ibtisam is a youth worker with her own children in local schools. She believes good services for under-fives gives children the best start in life. Ibtisam also wants to see an end to homeless families. She supports Labour’s policies to make our streets safer for women, girls and young men

Jackie is the lead councillor for championing better use of green spaces across Lambeth. She is also concerned that with so many services going online some people are left out. Training to use digital facilities is needed in the community. Jackie supports local businesses and the Station to Station business association.

Emma is passionate about improving the state of our local streets and tackling flytipping. Rubbish bin issues with bedsits (HMOs) are a growing problem addressed by proper licensing. She is pleased to see the improvements in controlling hired e-bikes and e-scooters. Emma is co-chair of Norwood’s Cemetery Committee and following the new gate to Hubbard Road is looking forward to another new pedestrian gate in Robson Road! As a school governor and local parent of two children Emma is aware of the challenges to school budgets; of reducing school rolls; and the increasing number of children with special educational needs.

Lambeth has been my home for seven years now, having moved down to London from Birkenhead for university.

Our community is a huge source of strength and dignity. From housing to health, I am motivated by how our community has risen up to the challenges, time and again.

I have experienced firsthand how poorly our community is served. When I asked for help in my own times of need, I saw how easy it was to slip through the net; particularly if you are from a minority background. I am standing to be your local champion because I know that we can - and must - do better.

In my working life, I do research and campaigns for charities to tackle the growing health inequalities, including access to mental health services for all.

I am here because I want to help. I want to see as responsive and inclusive local politics as our community in how it values and builds on the good that is already here; and speaks up for us when we need it. I hope you will give me that chance.

I’m Patience. I was born in Uganda and moved to the UK when I was nine. Soon after arriving, my life changed dramatically and I entered the care system. I remained in care until 21 and continued my education. My experiences in care shaped my values: compassion, resilience, and a determination to make sure people aren’t left to struggle alone.
One of the first children’s homes I lived in was in Myatt’s Field. Places like Marcus Lipton and Max Roach after-school clubs gave me structure, encouragement and a sense of belonging. I saw first-hand how youth services and safe community spaces can keep young people on positive paths—and how quickly things can unravel when that support isn’t there.
I’ve kept going with education even when it wasn’t easy. I studied Hair and Beauty at Lambeth College, then completed Painting and Decorating at Vauxhall College. I’m now studying Business Management at university and am in my penultimate year. Standing as a Green council candidate still feels surreal—but I know my background is a strength, not a barrier.
Advocacy has always been part of who I am. As a teenager, I was already helping other young people in care, navigate services and finding the right organisations for support. I grew up in Brixton, and I’m proud of what this community has given me. But I’ve also watched friends and long-standing residents pushed out by rising costs and regeneration that doesn’t feel like it’s for them. That’s why I want local decision-making that truly listens to residents—and backs it up with action.
If elected, I’ll focus on practical, people-first priorities:
•	Protecting and expanding youth services, and keeping community spaces open and funded
•	Standing up for care-experienced children and young people, with services that are easy to access and joined up
•	Fighting for genuinely affordable housing, better repairs, and fairness for tenants
•	Making sure housing improvements and refurbishments strengthens communities rather than displacing them

Annie was a local community activist long before becoming a councillor. As a former resident chair of the Cowley Estate Management Board, where she is a council tenant, she established the award-winning Cowley Food Farm.

Annie has a strong track record of advising residents on benefits and home office applications. She is passionate about improving the future opportunities of our young people and is a regular volunteer at Ebony Horse Club and Myatt’s Field Greenhouses.

John Lubbock is a journalist, former community organiser and charity worker, and part time student. He has lived in Myatt’s Fields for almost all of his life, and writes regularly about the housing crisis in Lambeth and the rest of London, and particularly the problem of temporary accommodation.

Working as a community organiser on Clapham Park Estate gave John first hand experience of advocating for people who are most affected by the housing crisis and Lambeth Labour’s failed estate regeneration projects, and he is keen to be a local champion for residents fighting for repairs on Myatt’s Fields South and against cuts to Parks and library budgets.

Tara Amfo was born and raised in Lambeth and grew up in a large, close-knit family where speaking up and supporting others was always encouraged.

She studied International Business, gaining insight into how culture shapes communication and understanding how different perspectives influence the way people interact and work together. Tara now works as an Occupational Health Technician, where she continues to support people’s wellbeing in practical and meaningful ways.

Tara has always had a strong sense of justice and community responsibility. While in sixth form, she worked with her peers to encourage the school to introduce an in-school counsellor after recognising the need for better mental health support for students.

Tara believes in making spaces accessible and welcoming for everyone. In a previous role, she even learned sign language to communicate with a regular customer, ensuring they felt included and valued.

Tara is from the community and for the community.

Marco is a local trade union activist, he works at an FE teacher in London. He is a member of the Socialist Party and involved in a campaign locally against councils cuts to children's services and housing. 
Lambeth Labour- led council has already inflicted brutal cuts to services. Now they are planning to make a further £90million! TUSC says, now the hated Tory government is gone, Labour controlled councils have no excuse for making more cuts. Instead councils should use their borrowing powers and reserves to fund the services that people in Lambeth desperately need. Then they should demand the national government picks up the tab. Lambeth has lost £200million in funding since 2010. We want that funding back!

Claire is a Labour candidate for Oval and has lived in the area for over 30 years, raising her family locally. As a councillor and the leader of Lambeth Labour, Claire has worked hard to support residents, listen to concerns and protect vital local services, particularly during years of government cuts. Previously a legal aid children’s lawyer and Chair of Governors at Ashmole Primary School, they have long campaigned for greater support and opportunities for young people. Claire is committed to continuing to serve Oval and building a stronger, safer neighbourhood for everyone.

Diogo is a Labour candidate for Oval and is standing for re-election as a local councillor. Having grown up in the area, the community has always been his home and remains at the heart of his work. During his first term, he has supported residents, strengthened the neighbourhood, and ensured that local voices, including those from migrant communities, are heard at the Town Hall. Alongside his council role, Diogo serves as a school governor at Wyvil Primary School and a trustee of Lambeth Links, an LGBT+ charity. He is committed to continuing to serve Oval and build on the progress made with residents.

Bryn Scott is a Labour candidate for Oval and lives on Richborne Terrace. A private renter, he cares deeply about the community and the issues that matter to local people. He previously worked for Lambeth Council, giving him a strong understanding of how to get things done, and before that supported local businesses to access council support during the pandemic. Bryn is committed to working with local representatives to deliver for residents, businesses and community organisations, and to keep Oval a great place to live.

As a long term Lambeth resident, I want a borough that works for everyone - fairer housing, safer streets, and a council that puts people first. As a leaseholder, I’ve experienced poor management firsthand and will push for competent, transparent, and compassionate housing services that treat residents properly.

Dez Germon has lived in Oval and Kennington wards for 25 years and volunteers for several charities

“In my time living here, I’ve volunteered for charities supporting migrants, homeless people, a food bank and I’m a volunteer gardener in Kennington Park.

I’m proud to support more social housing, rent controls, local venues, arts and nightlife and safe spaces for LGBTQ+ people.”

Pedro Xavier is a trilingual language specialist that works closely with the emigrant community in Lambeth.

He was awarded a degree in Bachelor of Arts in Teaching English as a Foreign Language with English Language & British Culture and Communication Studies.

Apart from the work that he now privately does as CEO of PLS Consultants International where he deals with a myriad of problems mostly relating to the language barrier, and runs an accountancy practice.

He worked as an Executive Officer at the DWP, teacher at both the University of Middlesex and Saint Francis Xavier Sixth Form College in London, a Local Education Authority Governor for Bishop Thomas Grant Secondary School and even an external consultant for the Portuguese Consulate in the United Kingdom.

He is also a Portuguese Councillor for the Portuguese communities in the UK, and advisory body to the Portuguese Government

In 2015 Pedro was nominated for the award of “Empreendorismo Inovador na Diáspora Portuguesa” that was given by the Portuguese President, where he was awarded the “Diploma de Mérito”.

He has spent thousands of hours translating and interpreting English, Portuguese and Spanish assisting the minorities within the Borough of Lambeth and has worked with many solicitor firms, both national and foreign, in dealing with a range of issues that arise in the community.

Whenever Pedro is not working at PLS Consultants or patrolling the streets as a Special Constabulary Sergeant at Metropolitan Police he is home spending time with his family and their two dogs

Hashi has lived in Stockwell for over 30 years, and a councillor since 2018. He has worked hard to help our most vulnerable residents secure housing of a good standard. He has also been a youth and community worker for over 20 years setting up numerous community projects including the Brixton soup kitchen and New Beginnings which carried out gang intervention programmes on several estates in Stockwell. He has worked closely with the police and local residents. He has challenged anti-social behaviour in Stockwell as a former secretary of the Safer Neighbourhood Panel, including as Chair of Lambeth Stop and Search monitoring group.

I moved to Stockwell eight years ago and it has now become my home. I’m a member of the Residents’ Committee for where I live — currently pursuing fire safety issues with MTVH Housing Association. I’m an active member of Friends of Larkhall Park and a volunteer litter picker. I also volunteer at a local COVID Vaccination Centre.

As a local school governor, NHS COVID vaccine volunteer and committed LGBT+ campaigner, I am passionate about supporting all our communities. I live on the Larkhall Estate and will always listen to your views as we seek to build a greener, fairer, and stronger Stockwell West & Larkhall.

Fawzia has lived and worked as an architect in the Kennington area for several decades, and was involved in setting up the Ethelred Estate TMO.

She says the construction industry needs to deal with its impact on climate change – concrete manufacture is one of the highest emitters of C02.

“Lambeth Labour is allowing the spread of large towers throughout Kennington, without care for the negative impacts on our environment.

A recent example? The Council-owned Denby Court has buildings that can be retrofitted, yet Lambeth Labour just gave permission for 2 large concrete towers, which will overshadow surrounding residents' homes and Lambeth Walk Doorstep Green.

I am standing to hold Lambeth Council to account.”

Fawzia Kane

Green Party candidate for Brixton North, Neil Sheppeck has recently been nominated for The Samantha Heath Award for London’s Changemaker of the Year for his “commitment, dedication and time given to the success of Lambeth’s Citizens Assembly,” and leading the What If Lambeth? project.

Sheppeck has lived in Lambeth for over two decades, and has worked tirelessly for social and environmental justice.

Sheppeck works for a SEND school in Lambeth and is specifically passionate about youth opportunities, environmental issues, and sustainable housing.

Vote for Neil Sheppeck. Vote for a greener and fairer Lambeth.

Door knocked 70% of our ward so far. Residents confirm I'm the only candidate at their doorstep. I've lived here 19 years and remain actively involved in Lansdowne Green residents' group, Friends of Oasis Nature Garden parents network, and everyday community life. This is my home, and I understand the issues local people face because I'm part of those same conversations every day. I'm standing as an independent to be a trusted, approachable councillor focused on housing, safety, parking, children services and helping with cost of living pressures, ensuring every part of the ward gets a fair hearing.

candidates.p: The Socialist Party of Great Britain
candidates.stmt:
**Socialism: Freedom, Equality, Solidarity**

Socialists are against authoritarianism, privilege and division, We are for freedom, equality, and solidarity. These can only be achieved in a society based on common ownership and democratic control by the whole people.

Freedom means genuine freedom of thought and expression as well as freedom from having to work for wages. We are fully committed to defending open debate and independent thinking. No government or corporation should silence the voices of working people or control how people think and live.

Equality means that every human being is of equal worth regardless of ethnicity, gender, or sexual orientation. We stand for a society of social coexistence based on mutual respect.

Solidarity is the shared commitment to live together with respect, cooperation, and collective responsibility. We stand with working people, defending unity over division and cooperation over conflict.

Capitalism works against these principles. We oppose this economic system that concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a few whilst exploiting the vast majority and that puts profits before people. We oppose the wars it inevitably generates over markets and resources.

We are not standing to manage capitalism locally. If elected, we will be an uncompromising voice for workers in every decision that affects them, making the case for a social revolution to replace capitalism with a society based on common ownership, democratic control, and production directly to meet people’s needs not profits.

In the course of his professional career, Nick worked successively in the civil service, management consultancy and investment management.  He is now a trustee of a charity based in South London and is active in local community theatre.  He and his family have lived in Streatham for over 25 years.

Nick became involved in politics again a few years ago following the Tories’ disastrous Brexit.  But he has also seen how important it is to elect councillors who will work for and listen to local residents, rather than imposing ill-thought-through policies on them against their wishes, as Lambeth Labour have so often done in recent years.

Claire has called Streatham home for nearly 20 years and loves the area’s energy and strong sense of community. Over the years, she has actively contributed to local life, and in 2008 co-founded the charity Love Streatham, helping to bring people together through community action days and family fun days on Streatham Common.

She lives locally with her husband in a leasehold flat where she has served on the residents’ association, giving her first-hand experience of representing neighbours and working constructively to resolve shared challenges. Claire believes everyone deserves good-quality, well-maintained, and affordable housing, as well as a meaningful voice in how their homes and communities are run. Professionally, she has worked in housing policy for several years.

Claire knows too many residents feel ignored. She wants a council that listens and acts. That’s why she’s standing for election — to make sure local voices are respected and to fight for safer streets and a cleaner environment.

Claire values fairness and respect, and believes these principles should be at the heart of every local decision. With enthusiasm and a genuine love for Streatham, she is ready to stand up for the community and help shape a future residents can be proud of.

Simon grew up in South London and has called Streatham home since 2012. A Lambeth Leaseholder Simon has experienced first hand how difficult it can be to get Lambeth to do even basic repairs and maintenance. Getting a better deal for tenants is a top priority who need a council that gets the basics right.

An experienced campaigner Simon has worked for MPs and in the charity sector to deliver change. He is standing for election as he wants to see a council that starts listening to residents again. We have big decisions like taking leisure services back to being directly run without users being consulted and little evidence made available on how this and many other aspects of Lambeth are performing.

It's time we had a council that listened to residents and put their priorities first rather than a council that rides roughshod over people's views. Working alongside Claire and Nick and the Lib Dem team Simon will make sure residents in Streatham Common and Vale can make their voice heard at the Town Hall.

Cllr Martin Abrams was elected as a Labour councillor in Streatham St Leonards in 2022. After being suspended for supporting a motion calling for a ceasefire in Gaza in January 2024, he joined Lambeth Green Party in 2025. He joined the Green Party as a logical continuation of his lifelong fight for social, economic and environmental justice.

Martin brings a wealth of experience. He is a committed, effective and hard-working councillor and a National Policy Officer at RMT Union. He works tirelessly to create a fundamental and permanent shift of wealth and power to the working class. Like many in the Green Party he advocates for the power of organising people and communities to build skills and knowledge. Constructing political and social power at a grassroots level to create systemic change for a fairer, greener more beautiful world.

Amelia Shoebridge grew up on the nearby Pullens Estate, where she saw the challenges we face – housing, cuts, inequality – but also how communities can work to challenge injustices.

Amelia’s work has focused on social impact and community engagement, including leading partnerships with environmental organisations. She leads volunteers and schools programmes, and previously worked with refugees in Brixton to help support them through our system. She believes strongly in a community-led, socially and environmentally just society – starting right here in Streatham Hill East.

Liz has lived in Streatham Hill for over 30 years. A councillor since 2014, she has worked hard to help local residents secure decent housing – ensuring repairs are carried out for council tenants, challenging rogue private landlords and supporting those facing homelessness. With the police and local residents she has tackled anti-social behaviour in Streatham Hill – from dealing with threatening neighbours to ensuring groups of aggressive street drinkers are moved on. Liz will continue to listen to local people prioritising their concerns and ensuring that their voices are heard by the Council. Her priority is to ensure Streatham Hill provides a safe, healthy and family friendly environment in which everyone can thrive.

Alex Davies grew up near Streatham Common station, attending Immanuel and St Andrew Primary School in Buckleigh Road. He has lived in Streatham for 34 years.

Alex joined the Lib Dems in 1999 to campaign against library closures in the area, and was elected as a councillor in 2010. He worked with residents to save Streatham Tate Library when Lambeth’s Labour Council threatened it with closure.

Rezina has lived in Streatham Hill for almost 20 years. She was active in the community prior to being elected through volunteering at her local school, campaigning for the development of the Megabowl, A23 traffic calming and tackling anti-social behaviour. She has helped those who would otherwise have struggled to have their voice heard, installing accessibility ramps for wheelchair users, parking bays for those with mobility issues, accelerating housing repairs or helping parents with school entry. Rezina will ensure council and leasehold residents are served well and challenge the poor state of repairs by contractors. She will continue to work to improve our community:more green spaces, safer streets, healthier public realm, supporting local businesses, better sports, and play at Hillside Gardens Park and cleaner air. Rezina will work hard to ensure the health and wellbeing of those in Streatham Hill by actively engaging with and listening to residents.

Scott Ainslie has lived in Streatham since 1997. He is originally from Edinburgh and studied Business Studies at Edinburgh Napier University before becoming a professional actor.

In 2009, Scott became a founding member of Sustainable Streatham. The group works to make the local area a safer, fairer, cleaner, greener place to live and work. He was also on the board of Streatham Action, the umbrella body for community groups in Streatham.

Scott was first elected to represent St Leonard’s Ward as the only green councillor to Lambeth Council in 2014. He was then re-elected in 2018 with an increased majority and proud to replace the Conservatives as the main opposition on the Council with four other elected Greens: making Lambeth home to the largest group of Green councillors in London.

In June 2019 he was also elected as Member of the European Parliament for London, winning the largest ever vote for a Green MEP candidate in the capital with 12.4% of the vote.

As a Councillor for Streatham St Leonard's Ward, Scott has gained a reputation for being one of Lambeth’s hardest working councillors – asking more questions on behalf of residents and being tenacious at getting things done. He fought alongside residents to defend their estates from demolition and compulsory 'regeneration'. He has worked to save local libraries from closure and to support culturally diverse independent businesses. Scott has also campaigned for justice for survivors of abuse in Lambeth's children’s homes. He also worked cross-party to secure the first ever successful motion to commit a local authority to support the call to set up an All-Party Parliamentary Commission of Inquiry for Truth and Reparatory Justice for the Transoceanic Trafficking of Enslaved Africans. He also helped ensure the motion was also passed with a large majority through the Green Party of England and Wales.

Scott believes Lambeth residents have a right to know how the Council spends public money. He helps the independent

I’ve seen the devastating impact of austerity on services and opportunities for young people.

As a youth worker supporting children and young people in Streatham, I’ve seen the devastating impact of austerity on services and opportunities for young people. I want to make Lambeth a thriving borough for all our communities and I believe this can happen if more Green Party councillors are elected next May.

Local leadership should be about people — compassion in action, fairness in every decision.

Through my work with SWAN and in local community projects, I’ve seen the strength of people coming together – from supporting families through food banks to empowering women and young people. I’m standing for the Greens because we deserve a council that listens, acts with integrity, and puts people first.

**Together we can fix Lambeth**

The Liberal Democrats have a plan to fix our Borough. Lambeth Labour have let us down. Labour say there isn’t enough money to keep the streetlights bright or run the Lambeth Country Show. But Labour have wasted millions on failed housing schemes and on fighting residents in court.

**1. Ensure residents shape decisions that affect them**
Require resident agreement and co-design for everything from traffic schemes and park events to care and special educational needs plans.
Bring back lost youth facilities with young people designing what they need.
End the ‘Town Hall knows best’ culture and Labour’s ‘tick-box’ consultations
**2. Fix Lambeth’s finances**
Redirect the millions Labour waste on failed vanity projects back into local services, ending the era of undeliverable financial 'wish-lists', bail-out loans and escalating debt.

**3. Tackling crime & caring for our streets**
Keep people safe by ending Labour’s street light dimming plan.
Partner with police on active CCTV monitoring in crime hot-spots.
Keep police station front counters open and promote trust in policing.
Plant more trees, repair pavements and clean streets.
Launch regular ‘mega-skip’ rounds, and catch and fine fly-tippers.
**4. Fix Lambeth Housing and tackle homelessness**
Redirect the £21m Labour is paying lawyers defending the council's shoddy repairs into actual repairs.
Pay contractors for finished jobs – not every call-out, with a tenant / leaseholder sign-off process before payment is made.
Build new social housing and bring empty homes back into use to tackle homelessness
**5. Improved care: safe, person-led and efficient**
Back home carers and involve people in their own care plans.
Protect children with early intervention with family support involving the wider family in children’s plans.
Support domestic abuse refuges.
Offer elderly and disabled people home safety checks to prevent greater risk and cost later.
**6. A fairer deal for everyone on our roads**

Over the 15 years, Jacqueline has worked as a political campaigner and science educator.

Jacqueline, who has lived in Vauxhall since 2017, stood as the Green Party candidate for Vauxhall in the 2019 General Election and the 2023 Vauxhall by-election. She campaigned against Brexit in 2015/16. In the General election 2019, Jacqueline standing as MP candidate for Vauxhall  achieved the highest percentage rise in the constituency (by proportion), increasing by 150% from the previous election.

Jacqueline wants politics led by the people, and has campaigned against pharmaceutical malpractice in a patient and politics-focused mental health charity, and fought for greater protection of the environment as a campaigner and volunteer at The Animal Fund (TAF), an environmental charity which operates across Europe. She started an environmental campaign group called United for Ocean last year which acts to bring together different groups to work together for environmental action to protect the ocean.

Jacqueline Bond says about Vauxhall:
"Our community deserves more than complacency in the council as we face the weight of an ever deteriorating cost of  living crisis, housing insecurity, and harmful pollution in Vauxhall.

I believe in the power of community-led change, which the Green Party stands for, and why I stand as a councillor to help locals by listening to them and acting accordingly. Now more than ever, we can challenge the entrenched norm that accepts a one-party Labour council that treads cautiously while our streets remain unaffordable, our homes uncertain, and air polluted.

With a renewed Lambeth Council with Green Party councillors we can implement policies that prioritise affordable housing and sustainable development. We can refund public services and put back into the community where it has been sold off or neglected. We want to address the lack of transparency in the budget and champion libraries, green spaces and safe, clean streets in the area."

I live just off Kennington Road and have served as a local councillor since being elected in 2022. I have been active in the community for several years, including as a trustee of a Vauxhall charity working to improve people’s lives. I'm also a trustee of a community centre charity in Kennington.

Professionally, I work in the City in financial services. I bring a practical and organised approach, with a focus on improving council services and communications and to reduce inefficiencies at Town Hall.

Locally I will focus on the issues residents raise most often, including tackling crime and antisocial behaviour, pushing for housing repairs to be carried out properly and on time, and working to keep our streets clean and safe.

Originally from Somerset, Nick has lived in South London for fifteen years and proudly called Herne Hill home for ten. His favourite place in the world is Brockwell Park, and his idea of heaven is a summer's afternoon basking on the hill. Nick has actively resisted the council's renting out of the land to festival promoters.

In his professional life, Nick is a journalist who writes mostly about the sport of professional cycling. A private renter who lives at Dorchester Court, Nick has supported the campaign to force the owner to repair its grand old buildings, while opposing the planned development which would destroy the strong community that exists there.

Denean is proud to be a life-long Lambeth resident, and part of the diverse and vibrant communities that call Lambeth home. She is concerned about health and housing disparities that the recent pandemic highlighted and has worked with other residents to keep Lambeth’s streets safe and clean for all.

I was born in Bethnal Green Hospital and my family are from Tower Hamlets. I worked as a civil servant for cabinet ministers and in Parliament and subsequently represented the energy industry in media, government and Parliaments. A key part of this was championing the role for gas in our energy mix. As a result I am a strong critic of Tory Net Zero policy and don’t believe we should be ripping out our gas boilers.

I’m also standing for the Greater London Assembly for the London North East constituency comprising the London Boroughs of Hackney, Islington and Waltham Forest. 

I would bin all the unnecessary traffic restrictions imposed across London. With violent crime rising I believe the police should be policing our streets not social media.

I’m appalled that the sitting Poplar and Limehouse Labour MP has aligned herself with extremist causes like Hamas and has sort division in our community not unity.
